summary: Jeff Atwood challenges the common practice of adding namespaces to community-contributed code, arguing that most namespaces serve no functional purpose. He observes that while .NET's system namespaces are well-organized, community code namespaces are chaotic and often just combinations of random words. Atwood proposes that developers should skip namespaces entirely for most shared code, since they rarely solve actual collision problems. He concludes that namespaces on small projects are essentially vanity license plates that add complexity without benefit.
key_points: Community-contributed code namespaces are anarchistic and inconsistent, often using random word combinations like 'monkeyCode' or 'fuzzyAlienWare'
Most namespaces don't solve any actual collision or duplication problems—they're purely cosmetic
Namespaces only make sense for signed binaries with no source code; otherwise they're unnecessary overhead
Developers should focus on giving classes good descriptive names rather than adding namespace prefixes
The practice of adding namespaces 'just because' represents blind adherence to convention without considering actual needs
money_quotes: I have a modest proposal for you: how about no namespace at all?
Almost every time I see namespaces used, they're not functional. They don't solve any collision or duplication problems for me.
They're little more than vanity license plates for the author's code.
Community / open source code namespaces are an anarchistic babel.
Stop with the veiled ego tripping, give your class a good descriptive name, and have the cojones to leave it at that.
How many MhtBuilder classes are out there that I need to distinguish between?
key_insight: Namespaces in community code are often just developer vanity rather than solving real organizational problems—if there's no actual collision to prevent, skip the namespace entirely.
